description:
You are a log analyst tasked with a very small “triage-style” network investigation.

  1. The raw data you must work with is already present in  
     /home/user/logs/network_traffic.log  
     Every line is in the following fixed, space-separated format:  

        ISO-8601-timestamp  SRC_IP  ->  DST_IP  BYTES  

     Example line (the actual file has many of them):  
        2024-05-01T10:00:01Z 192.168.1.10 -> 10.0.0.5 450

  2. Your objectives (do **all** of them with normal shell utilities; 3-4 commands are enough):
     a. Calculate how many **unique** source IP addresses appear in the log.  
     b. Compute the total number of bytes transmitted **per source IP** and determine the **top 3 talkers** (the three source IPs that transferred the most bytes, highest first).  
     c. Write your findings to a brand-new file called  
        /home/user/logs/analysis/traffic_summary.txt  

        The file must contain exactly **five** lines, no extra spaces or blank lines, and follow *precisely* this layout:

           Unique source IPs: <integer>
           Top 3 talkers:
           1. <ip1> (<bytes1> bytes)
           2. <ip2> (<bytes2> bytes)
           3. <ip3> (<bytes3> bytes)

        • Replace the tokens in angle brackets with the real values you calculated.  
        • Keep the literal words, colons, parentheses, single spaces, and line breaks unchanged.

  3. After writing the file, display its contents in the terminal so the reviewer can immediately see the result.

  Notes & constraints:
  • Create the directory /home/user/logs/analysis if it does not already exist; you do **not** need root rights.  
  • The whole task can be done with a small pipeline of awk / sort / uniq or similar—no external programming languages are required.  
  • Be sure not to alter /home/user/logs/network_traffic.log itself.  
  • This task will be auto-graded: the grader will read /home/user/logs/analysis/traffic_summary.txt and compare it byte-for-byte to the expected text, so formatting accuracy is essential.
truth:
The file /home/user/logs/network_traffic.log is pre-populated with the following 11 lines exactly (newline after each line):

2024-05-01T10:00:01Z 192.168.1.10 -> 10.0.0.5 450
2024-05-01T10:00:02Z 192.168.1.20 -> 10.0.0.5 1500
2024-05-01T10:00:03Z 192.168.1.10 -> 10.0.0.5 600
2024-05-01T10:00:04Z 192.168.1.30 -> 10.0.0.5 200
2024-05-01T10:00:05Z 192.168.1.20 -> 10.0.0.5 700
2024-05-01T10:00:06Z 192.168.1.20 -> 10.0.0.5 800
2024-05-01T10:00:07Z 192.168.1.40 -> 10.0.0.5 50
2024-05-01T10:00:08Z 192.168.1.30 -> 10.0.0.5 300
2024-05-01T10:00:09Z 192.168.1.10 -> 10.0.0.5 200
2024-05-01T10:00:10Z 192.168.1.50 -> 10.0.0.5 1000
2024-05-01T10:00:11Z 192.168.1.20 -> 10.0.0.5 500

  Ground-truth metrics derived from that file:
    • Total unique source IPs: 5
    • Per-IP byte totals:
        192.168.1.20 = 3500
        192.168.1.10 = 1250
        192.168.1.50 = 1000
        192.168.1.30 = 500
        192.168.1.40 = 50

    • Therefore the required file /home/user/logs/analysis/traffic_summary.txt must contain EXACTLY:

Unique source IPs: 5
Top 3 talkers:
1. 192.168.1.20 (3500 bytes)
2. 192.168.1.10 (1250 bytes)
3. 192.168.1.50 (1000 bytes)

  Automated tests will:
    1. Verify that /home/user/logs/analysis/traffic_summary.txt exists.
    2. Confirm its contents are byte-for-byte identical to the 5-line block above (including newline at end of final line).
    3. (Optional) Check that the agent echoed the same contents to STDOUT at least once.
